refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Derivation is the primary functional obligation in the problem domain; recording and managing are implementation concerns that belong in the DP/IM layers. The refined FR focuses on the core functional need: translating mission context into performance targets. This remains solution-neutral and avoids prescribing 'method,' 'document,' or 'history' which are design choices.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that given a worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety constraints from stakeholders), the system produces a traceable set of performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that satisfy those inputs. Verify traceability from worksite inputs to derived requirements.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall capture worksite mission context inputs (e.g., production targets, site constraints, safety policies).
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements (e.g., cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) from captured worksite mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall record derived performance requirements with traceability to source mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance requirement baselines and calibration updates.

candidate_dps: DP-NGHE-005.1: Establish a parameterized mission-context capture mechanism that ingests worksite operational inputs (safety plan constraints, mission objectives, environmental conditions) and maps them to a standardized performance requirement template, with version control and audit trail.

DP-NGHE-005.2: Implement a rule-based inference engine that translates worksite mission parameters (hazard classification, operational tempo, personnel density, equipment constraints) into quantified performance targets via a documented decision matrix, with traceability links to source inputs.

DP-NGHE-005.3: Design a hierarchical baseline registry that stores performance target snapshots, calibration deltas, and justification records, enabling rollback and historical comparison of requirement evolution against worksite context changes.

DP-NGHE-005.4: Establish a federated data model where worksite mission context (safety plan, policies, operational constraints) is maintained as a separate authoritative source, with NGHE performance derivation as a read-only consumer that generates derived targets on demand with explicit dependency tracking.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-NGHE-005.2 + DP-NGHE-005.3 (combined): Implement a rule-based inference engine that translates worksite mission parameters into quantified performance targets via a documented decision matrix with traceability, coupled with a hierarchical baseline registry that maintains performance target snapshots, calibration deltas, and justification records. This combination satisfies both the derivation requirement (FR logic) and the documented history/traceability requirement (acceptance criteria) while maintaining separation of concerns.

potential_sources: Tight coupling risk: If the rule-based inference engine is hardcoded to specific worksite policy formats, changes to safety plan structure will require engine modification. Mitigation: Use a schema-agnostic parameter binding layer.
Moderate coupling risk: Baseline registry and inference engine must share a common performance target data model. Mitigation: Define a canonical IM-level schema contract with versioning.
Low coupling risk: Decision matrix maintenance (rule updates) vs. historical baseline immutability. Mitigation: Treat decision matrix as configuration, not code; baseline registry as append-only.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP pair avoids naming specific inference technologies (e.g., Drools, OWL) and keeps the solution at the logical level. Separation between context ingestion (DP-NGHE-005.1 responsibility, delegated to worksite authority) and derivation logic (DP-NGHE-005.2) supports independence. The registry (DP-NGHE-005.3) is orthogonal to derivation and can be implemented independently.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context (safety plan, operational policies) is provided in a structured or semi-structured format that can be parsed and mapped to the performance requirement template. RISK: If worksite inputs are unstructured (free-form documents), the inference engine will require significant natural language processing or manual interpretation, increasing cost and error rate.

ASSUMPTION: A decision matrix (rules mapping mission parameters to performance targets) can be defined and validated with worksite stakeholders before deployment. RISK: If mission context is highly variable or novel, the decision matrix may be incomplete or incorrect, leading to derived targets that do not reflect actual operational needs.

ASSUMPTION: Calibration updates and baseline changes are infrequent enough to be managed through a versioned registry. RISK: If performance targets must be re-derived in real-time as mission context changes dynamically, the registry approach becomes a bottleneck; a streaming/reactive architecture may be required instead.

ASSUMPTION: Traceability from derived performance targets back to source worksite inputs is a primary acceptance criterion. RISK: If the decision matrix is complex or uses intermediate derived parameters, traceability chains may become opaque, requiring additional metadata and governance overhead.

ASSUMPTION: The NGHE system is a consumer of worksite mission context, not an authoritative source. RISK: If NGHE performance targets are used to inform or override worksite safety policies, bidirectional coupling and conflict resolution logic will be required.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR-NGHE-005 (refined): The NGHE solution shall derive quantified performance targets from worksite mission context (safety plan, operational policies, environmental constraints) using a documented rule-based mapping, maintain a versioned baseline registry of performance target snapshots with calibration justifications, and provide end-to-end traceability from each derived target to its source mission parameters and decision rule. The system shall support rollback to prior baselines and audit trails of all target changes. Acceptance criteria: (1) Performance targets are documented and traceable to worksite inputs via explicit decision matrix rules; (2) Baseline history is maintained with timestamps, calibration deltas, and justification records; (3) Traceability links are queryable and auditable; (4) System supports comparison of target evolution across baseline versions.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Mission planning and performance target derivation layer that houses the rule-based inference engine, decision matrix, and hierarchical baseline registry for worksite-specific performance calibration and traceability.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives worksite mission parameters (location, task type, environmental constraints, operational objectives) to drive inference engine rule evaluation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs derived performance targets and baseline snapshots to Vehicle and Teleoperation for execution monitoring and adaptive control.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and boundary conditions; outputs safety-qualified performance targets and justification records for Safety Services validation.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest cohesion: inference engine, decision matrix, and baseline registry are all mission-planning abstractions that logically belong in the solution layer. Minimal diffusion: only exports derived targets and traceability records via well-defined interfaces (Tasking in, Telemetry/SafetyAlert out). No algorithmic leakage into Vehicle or Teleoperation control loops.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical/control execution module. Placing inference logic and baseline registry here violates separation of concerns. The rule engine and decision matrix are mission-level abstractions, not vehicle-level control. Would require Vehicle to own mission planning logic, creating tight coupling and diffusion of concerns across hardware and software boundaries.

Weak fit: Teleoperation is an operator interface and command translation layer. While it consumes performance targets, it should not own the inference engine or baseline registry. Placing mission-planning logic here conflates operator interaction with mission derivation, reducing modularity and making the system harder to test and maintain independently.

Poor fit: Safety Services is a constraint-validation and alert-generation module. It should consume performance targets and validate them against safety boundaries, not generate them. Placing the inference engine and baseline registry here inverts the dependency and couples safety logic to mission planning, violating the principle that safety constraints should be inputs to, not owners of, performance derivation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner of the combined DP (inference engine + baseline registry) because: (1) it is the mission-planning abstraction layer responsible for translating worksite parameters into system-level performance targets; (2) the rule-based decision matrix and hierarchical baseline registry are mission-planning artifacts, not vehicle control, operator interface, or safety constraint logic; (3) all inputs (Tasking: mission parameters) and outputs (Telemetry: derived targets; SafetyAlert: safety-qualified targets and justification) flow through well-defined interfaces without algorithmic diffusion; (4) this placement preserves separation of concerns and enables independent testing, evolution, and reuse of the inference engine across different vehicle platforms and teleoperation modes.
containment_risks: Risk: If the inference engine rule set becomes vehicle-specific (e.g., rules that depend on Vehicle kinematics or actuator limits), diffusion into Vehicle module may become necessary. Mitigation: Enforce that rules operate only on mission-level abstractions (task type, environmental class, objective priority) and that vehicle-specific constraints are injected as inputs, not embedded in rules.

Risk: If the baseline registry grows to include real-time performance telemetry and adaptive recalibration, the boundary between NGHE Solution (planning) and Vehicle (execution) may blur. Mitigation: Clearly separate planning-time baseline snapshots (owned by NGHE Solution) from runtime performance tracking (owned by Vehicle or Telemetry aggregation layer).

Risk: If Safety Services requires deep inspection of inference logic to validate target derivation, tight coupling may emerge. Mitigation: Ensure SafetyAlert interface includes justification records and decision trace so Safety Services can validate without owning the inference engine.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-NGHE-005.2 + DP-NGHE-005.3 (combined) directly and sufficiently addresses FR-NGHE-005. The rule-based inference engine translates worksite mission parameters into quantified performance targets (derivation requirement), and the hierarchical baseline registry with documented decision matrix and traceability records satisfies the acceptance criterion for documented, traceable performance targets with historical calibration records. The combination is logically sound and non-redundant.

diffusion_risks: Vehicle-specific rule creep: If inference engine rules become dependent on vehicle kinematics, actuator limits, or hardware-specific constraints, the DP will diffuse into the Vehicle module. Current containment score (0.92) assumes mission-level abstraction only. Mitigation: Enforce rule authoring standards that prohibit vehicle-specific logic; inject vehicle constraints as external inputs to the inference engine.

Baseline registry scope drift: If the baseline registry expands to include real-time performance telemetry and adaptive recalibration during mission execution, the boundary between NGHE Solution (planning-time) and Vehicle/Telemetry (runtime) will blur. Current design assumes planning-time snapshots only. Mitigation: Explicitly separate planning-time baseline snapshots (NGHE Solution ownership) from runtime performance tracking (Vehicle or dedicated Telemetry aggregation layer).

Safety validation coupling: If Safety Services requires introspection of inference engine logic to validate target derivation (beyond consuming justification records), tight coupling will emerge and DP ownership may become ambiguous. Current design assumes SafetyAlert interface provides sufficient traceability. Mitigation: Ensure justification records and decision traces are complete and machine-readable; Safety Services validates outputs, not logic.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct IM owner with high cohesion (0.92) and minimal diffusion (0.08). The inference engine and baseline registry are mission-planning abstractions that logically belong in the solution layer, not in Vehicle (control execution), Teleoperation (operator interface), or Safety Services (constraint validation). All external interfaces (Tasking in, Telemetry/SafetyAlert out) are well-defined and do not leak algorithmic responsibility. The module boundary is clear and defensible provided the three diffusion risks above are actively managed through design standards and interface contracts.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The NGHE Solution module has clear input/output interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) and produces auditable artifacts (decision matrix, baseline snapshots, justification records). Verification can be performed by: (1) tracing worksite mission parameters through the inference engine to derived targets; (2) validating that all performance targets are documented in the baseline registry with calibration deltas and justification; (3) confirming that SafetyAlert outputs include decision traces for independent safety validation. Acceptance criteria (documented, traceable performance targets with historical baselines) are directly verifiable against NGHE Solution outputs.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The NGHE Solution module is unambiguously responsible for deriving performance requirements from worksite mission context. Ownership boundaries are well-defined: NGHE Solution owns the inference engine and baseline registry; Vehicle owns execution and runtime telemetry; Teleoperation owns operator interface; Safety Services owns constraint validation. No overlapping responsibilities or ambiguous handoffs detected. The module containment hypothesis is sound and supported by the interface design.

recommended_revisions: Formalize rule authoring standards document: Specify that inference engine rules must operate exclusively on mission-level abstractions (task type, environmental class, objective priority, safety constraints) and prohibit embedding of vehicle-specific logic (kinematics, actuator limits, hardware configuration). Vehicle-specific constraints must be injected as external inputs to the inference engine, not hardcoded in rules.

Define baseline registry scope boundary: Explicitly separate planning-time baseline snapshots (owned by NGHE Solution, generated at mission planning time) from runtime performance tracking and adaptive recalibration (owned by Vehicle or a dedicated Telemetry aggregation layer). Document the handoff point and data flow between planning and execution phases.

Enhance SafetyAlert interface specification: Ensure that all performance target outputs include complete justification records and decision traces (rule chain, parameter values, constraint checks) in machine-readable format. This allows Safety Services to validate target derivation without requiring introspection of inference engine logic, preserving module independence.

Add diffusion monitoring checkpoint: During detailed design and implementation, conduct a design review focused on the three identified diffusion risks (vehicle-specific rule creep, baseline registry scope drift, safety validation coupling). Use the containment scores and diffusion metrics as acceptance gates for code review and architectural audits.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence and clear module ownership. However, the high safety criticality and three identified diffusion risks (vehicle-specific rule creep, baseline registry scope drift, safety validation coupling) warrant decomposition of FR-NGHE-005 into sub-FRs that explicitly address containment and safety traceability. Decomposition will force design decisions about rule authoring standards, baseline registry boundaries, and SafetyAlert interface completeness at the functional level, making diffusion risks visible and manageable before detailed design. This is a safety-driven decomposition, not a remediation of gate failures.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-005.1: Derive performance targets from mission parameters using mission-level abstractions only (task type, environmental class, objective priority, safety constraints), with explicit prohibition of vehicle-specific logic embedding.

FR-NGHE-005.2: Maintain a planning-time baseline registry with documented calibration deltas, historical performance records, and clear separation from runtime performance tracking and adaptive recalibration.

FR-NGHE-005.3: Generate complete justification records and decision traces (rule chain, parameter values, constraint checks) for all derived performance targets in machine-readable format to enable independent safety validation without inference engine introspection.

FR-NGHE-005.4: Enforce rule authoring standards and design contracts that prevent diffusion of vehicle-specific constraints, baseline registry scope, and safety validation logic into NGHE Solution module boundaries.

assumptions: Mission-level abstractions (task type, environmental class, objective priority, safety constraints) are sufficient to derive performance targets without vehicle-specific logic.
Planning-time baseline snapshots and runtime performance tracking can be cleanly separated at a well-defined handoff point.
SafetyAlert interface can be enhanced to include complete justification records without requiring Safety Services to introspect inference engine logic.
Rule authoring standards and design contracts are enforceable through code review and architectural audits during detailed design.
High safety criticality justifies explicit decomposition to surface and manage diffusion risks early.
open_risks: Vehicle-specific rule creep: Inference engine rules may become dependent on vehicle kinematics, actuator limits, or hardware-specific constraints if rule authoring standards are not enforced. Mitigation: Formalize rule authoring standards document and conduct design review focused on this risk during detailed design.

Baseline registry scope drift: Baseline registry may expand to include real-time performance telemetry and adaptive recalibration during mission execution, blurring boundary between NGHE Solution (planning-time) and Vehicle/Telemetry (runtime). Mitigation: Explicitly define baseline registry scope boundary and document handoff point between planning and execution phases.

Safety validation coupling: Safety Services may require introspection of inference engine logic to validate target derivation, creating tight coupling and ambiguous ownership. Mitigation: Enhance SafetyAlert interface to include complete justification records and decision traces in machine-readable format, allowing Safety Services to validate outputs without logic introspection.

Justification record completeness: Justification records may be incomplete or not machine-readable, limiting Safety Services' ability to perform independent validation. Mitigation: Define justification record schema and validation requirements as part of SafetyAlert interface specification.

Design review effectiveness: Diffusion monitoring checkpoint during detailed design may not catch all instances of rule creep or scope drift if review criteria are not sufficiently rigorous. Mitigation: Use containment scores and diffusion metrics as quantitative acceptance gates for code review and architectural audits.
evidence_needed: Formalized rule authoring standards document specifying mission-level abstraction requirements and prohibitions on vehicle-specific logic.
Baseline registry scope boundary definition with explicit separation of planning-time snapshots from runtime performance tracking.
Enhanced SafetyAlert interface specification with justification record schema and machine-readable decision trace format.
Design review checklist focused on the three identified diffusion risks, with quantitative containment score targets (e.g., maintain containment ≥ 0.90).
Code review guidelines for detecting vehicle-specific rule creep, baseline registry scope drift, and safety validation coupling during implementation.
Traceability matrix linking sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-005.1 through FR-NGHE-005.4) to DPs and IMs, with explicit diffusion risk mitigation strategies for each sub-FR.
